did.physdesc: 1 external hard drive (4.24 gigabytes), 25 CD-Rs (7.55 gigabytes), 3 internal hard drives (3.75 gigabytes), 39 3.5 inch floppy disks (0.05 gigabytes)
did.abstract: The Tom Veitch papers consist of correspondence, writings, journals and notebooks, administrative and research files, photographs, artworks, audiovisual and computer media documenting the personal and professional activities of American writer Tom Veitch.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into nine series: I. Correspondence, 1967-2020. II. Writings, circa 1950-2022. III. Journals, 1962-2020. IV. Notebooks, 1963-2019. V. Administrative and research files, 1930-2020. VI. Works by others, circa 1971-2016. VII. Photographs, circa 1941-2019. VIII. Artworks, prints, and ephemera, circa 1970-2001. IX. Audiovisual and computer media, circa 1980-2022.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Tom Veitch (1941-2022) was an American comic writer, poet, novelist, and biographer. Veitch is widely known for his professional activities involving mainstream, commercial comics – especially regarding the Star Wars franchise during the 1990s.

Born in 1941, Veitch spent his youth in Walpole, New Hampshire, and Bellows Falls, Vermont. He moved to New York in 1959 to attend Columbia University, where he would ultimately drop out in 1962. Veitch began making connections with other writers during this period in New York. Collaborative relationships marked by poetic experimentation ensued between Veitch and members of the Second-Generation New York School, including Ron Padgett, Dick Gallup, Anne Waldman, Ted Berrigan, and Joe Brainard. Veitch would start publishing his work in 1964, attracting the attention of William S. Burroughs and Allen Ginsberg in the process. Following a 1968 move to San Francisco, Veitch increasingly participated in the Underground Comix movement. Much of his work with comics during this period was done in collaboration with Greg Irons. Veitch's varied output of prose, poetry, and editorship – exemplified in Tom Veitch Magazine (1970-1972) – attest to his eclectic and evolving literary activities.

The 1980s saw Veitch gradually transition into writing mainstream comics for major publishers like Marvel Comics and DC Comics. The Light & Darkness War (1988), a science fiction adventure published by Marvel's Epic Comics, made an impression on George Lucas. What followed was an extensive collaboration between Veitch, Lucasfilm, and Dark Horse Comics to produce new comics for the Star Wars franchise. Major works resulting from this collaboration include Dark Empire (1991-1992) , Dark Empire II (1994-1995), Tales of the Jedi (1993-1994), Tales of the Jedi: Dark Lords of the Sith (1994-1995), and Empire's End (1995).

Much of Veitch's literary work in the twenty-first century leaned heavily into themes of esotericism and religiosity. An omnipresent influence throughout his career, Veitch's spiritual life featured a brief tenure as a Benedictine monk in Vermont (1965-1967), decades of dream journaling rooted in Jungian psychology, and a sustained interest in esoteric traditions such as alchemy. In the 2000s, Veitch channeled these existing interests into his "Lightmind Forum," a public space on the internet for discussing spiritual concepts, and later into a book-length biography entitled Visions of Elias: A True Story in the Life of Spirit (Sky River Books, 2016). Veitch often used the pseudonym "Elias Oz" for his esoteric and spiritual writings. Veitch passed away in 2022 as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 99-101 (audiovisual): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Boxes [TBD] (born digital): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Tom Veitch papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.
Copies of commercially produced audiovisual materials contained in this collection cannot be made for researcher use outside of the repository.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Granary Books, Inc. on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2024.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Tom Veitch Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Significant portions of the collection have been rearranged by the archivist to promote usability. Original file titles have been retained and are enclosed in quotation marks wherever they appear in descriptions. Proper titles of works have been italicized.

Prior to the library's acquisition of Veitch's papers in 2024, staff at Granary Books, Inc. conducted a preliminary survey of the collection contents. The results of this survey informed the current arrangement and, in many cases, have been adapted for file-level Scope and Contents notes. Information in these file-level Scope and Contents notes is intended to enhance searchability and provide useful context for patrons. This information has not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases.

Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Yale Archival Processing Manual.
Series IX, containing audiovisual and computer media, is undergoing processing and will be made available for research as soon as possible.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Ron Padgett papers (YCAL MSS 721); Dick Gallup papers (YCAL MSS 1630).
A selection of comics and other periodicals by Veitch are available for research and can be requested in the catalog.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Tom Veitch papers consist of correspondence, writings, journals and notebooks, administrative and research files, photographs, artworks, audiovisual and computer media documenting the personal and professional activities of American writer Tom Veitch.

The collection's contents predominantly document Veitch's career as a comic book writer, the bulk of which spans 1960s-era drafts of independent comics to 1990s-era production files of mainstream science fiction works published by Marvel, DC, and Dark Horse Comics. Writings comprising Series II contain the largest proportion of files related to comic projects. Series I, IV, V, VIII, and computer media within Series IX also contain notable selections of materials relevant to these activities.

Other literary genres that Veitch worked in are documented in Series II and III. These encompass journals, poetry, biographies, and novels. The journals and poetry are also representative of Veitch's spirituality and dream analysis, which are further documented by the audiovisual media within Series IX.

Veitch's personal activities are most prominently reflected in the photographs comprising Series VII, with notable selections also being present in Series I, administrative notebooks within Series IV, and Series V. Due to the personal relationships Veitch maintained with long-term professional collaborators, Veitch's personal and professional activities often intermingle within the materials in each series. Such is evidenced in early collaborations with Ron Padgett, Dick Gallup, and Greg Irons as it is in later endeavors with Cam Kennedy.
Series IX, containing audiovisual and computer media, is undergoing processing and will be made available for research as soon as possible.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into two subseries: General correspondence and Star Wars .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains Veitch's incoming and outgoing correspondence spanning the late 1960s-2020.
The General correspondence subseries identifies files by named correspondents. The bulk of these materials span the 1960s-1990s. Topics include both personal and professional matters.

Rick Veitch – Tom Veitch's brother and a comic book writer and illustrator – is the most extensive correspondent in this subseries, with letters ranging from 1972-1996 that feature commentary on each other's literary works and discussions on personal matters. Additional major correspondents include incoming and outgoing letters from visual artist and comic illustrator Cam Kennedy, richly illustrated incoming letters from poet and visual artist Britton Wilkie, and incoming letters from editor, comic illustrator, and author Stephen Bissette.

Veitch sometimes corresponded with and about poets associated with the Second-Generation New York School, such as Anne Waldman, Britton Wilkie, Ron Padgett, and Dick Gallup. Letters with Padgett and Gallup appear in other series, especially within files for collaborative projects in Series II (e.g. project files for Antlers in the Treetops , Star Gut , and The Planetary Route ).
Brief files of incoming correspondence from Allen Ginsberg and William S. Burroughs are also present in General correspondence.

The Star Wars subseries contains business correspondence from the early to mid-1990s, coinciding with the height of Veitch's work authoring Star Wars comics. Correspondence includes incoming and outgoing letters with editors at Dark Horse Comics, Lucasfilm, and LucasArts. Projects discussed include Tales of the Jedi , Dark Empire , and Dark Empire II . Also present is incoming and outgoing correspondence with Kevin Anderson, at the time Veitch's co-author for the Tales of the Jedi: Dark Lords of the Sith serialization.
Lists of Veitch's personal and professional contacts are available in an address book in Series IV and a Rolodex in Series V. Email correspondence is available in Series IX.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Originally labeled "DIGITAL ANIMATION."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, Xeroxes of correspondence between Stephen R. (Steve) Bissette and others, original drawings, Xeroxes of drawings, drafts of projects, and other materials related to this American comic book artist specializing in the horror genre, who collaborated with Veitch on "Grumm" and other projects.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 7 pp. email exchange on Buddhist discourse, with Aneila Z, Zenmar, and Namdrol as correspondents. Related to Elias Oz and Lightmind Forum.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Originally labeled "BURBEY - DREAMER FILM."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Xerox copies of drawings and drafts of creator agreements with accompanying correspondence, regarding Burdine's illustration of Veitch comic (unpublished), "The Image War."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Packet of 5 letters, including typescript letters and autograph cards, from W.S. Burroughs to Veitch, most accompanied by envelopes. Carefully cataloged by Veitch himself in accompanying sheet. Dates span from Veitch's time in the Weston Priory to 1974, when he and Burroughs were discussing the possibility of a comic book adaptation of "Naked Lunch." Burroughs and Veitch got along well and remained in communication over the years; Veitch wrote an essay about him and their relationship in his later years which remains unpublished and is included in the archive.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 autograph letters, signed, from Allen Ginsberg (1971 and 1976), one of which is accompanied by a full-page illustration of "Planet News" and inscribed to Veitch and family.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Originally labelled "CAM - correspondence."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Working folder with incoming and outgoing correspondence between Tom Veitch and Denis Kitchen of Kitchen Sink Press, including annotated scripts for consideration, business and brainstorming correspondence, and some related ephemera.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mostly incoming correspondence, including publishing solicitations and other project-related activities. Includes thank-you autograph letter, signed, from Joseph Campbell in 1975, typewritten letter, signed, from Aram Saroyan in 1967, 2 autograph notes, signed, from Robert Creeley in 1971, 3 autograph letters, signed, and 1 typewritten letter, signed, from Philip Whalen (Kyoto, 1971, with explicit reflections on the small press publishing scene, and the statement "what the fuck is wrong with you babies" regarding Veitch and Aram Saroyan's recent magazines that he had encountered)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, typescript, and printed email correspondence with De Vore, Angelica Veitch (birthday card), Johnny Stanton, G.B. Trudeau, Pegasus Publishing, Graham Higgins (incoming and outgoing), Jason Colbey ("Blackout"), Stanley Arcieri (also signed "Order"), Bill Berkson, Shepherd Hendrix, and others. Some mail is addressed to Elias Oz.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 folders of correspondence, typescript and printed drafts, and holograph notes between Veitch and Rolf Mohr. At the time of this correspondence, he was an environment artist for DreamWorks Studios. Together, they were developing a science fiction film project titled "Devastator."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Outgoing letter to Denis Kitchen regarding Judge Dredd, packet of information protesting Bubba Free John's cult with letter by Veitch, and highly decorated envelope by William Ransonne with 2 pp. typewritten letter, signed, dream reflection.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black binder, titled "Letters to Rick 1972–1982," with over 200 original letters, mostly typescripts from Veitch to Rick Veitch, dated at top of letter (extending to 1985) in red ink, likely at time of binding. Approx. 29 pp. are incoming from Rick Veitch. Topics include comics and alchemy, "Star Wars," ongoing projects, dream interpretation, Veitch's "Visions of a Poet" manuscript, comic collaborations, and other discussions. Includes illustrated pencil and ink panel by Rick Veitch laid in, photograph of Tom, Martha, and parents, and some typescript poems. Includes copy of typewritten letter, signed, to Anne Waldman with accompanying poem submissions about which she expressed hesitation at publishing in 1980. Towards the end of the binder, there is back-and-forth correspondence about the dissolution of the Veitch brothers' collaborations and friendship circa 1984–1985.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder with autograph letter, signed, from Rick Veitch, containing nos. 355 and 356 of "Sgt Rock," which was lettered by Rick Veitch. In original mailing envelope.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Several autograph letters back and forth between Veitch and someone selling car parts. During the 1980s, Veitch was an auto mechanic and an editor for "Hemmings Motor News." There are several photographs of Veitch working on cars in the archive.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder labeled by Veitch. Two autograph letters, signed, from Anne Waldman in September 1986 regarding "Out of This World" Poetry Project Anthology, including administrative files for contributors and a clipping.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 68 pp. of autograph letters from Britton Wilke to Tom Veitch, from 1970–1975. Richly illustrated with pencil, watercolor, colored pencil, ink, and some collage. Extensive and elaborate letters, discussing potential collaborations, artistic influences, and ongoing projects. Accompanied 4 original drawings by Wilkie for "Tom Veitch Magazine," including the cover. With a hand-lettered "Malleus Maleficarum," as well as two astrological volvelles with moving parts. Illustration heavily inspired by medieval illuminated and alchemical traditions, and a beautiful example of Wilkie's talents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila folder titled "The Letters," with typescript, carbon typescript, and autograph letters, three-hole punched and arranged by Veitch. There are 20 pp. of letters with some additional ephemera; many are outgoing carbon or Xeroxed typescripts to "A," "Annie" [Anne Waldman?], "Britt[on Wilkie]," Rick Veitch, Clark Coolidge, "Al[len Appel," "Larryoskt [Larry Fagin]," Michael Brownstein, and others. Includes incoming notes from Allen Appel and Michael Brownstein, and a few pages of collaged works likely from "The Words."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: See computer media in Series IX.

did.unittitle: Dark Empire and Tales of the Jedi correspondence with Lucasfilm and Dark Horse Comics

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing approx. 2 linear inches of Xeroxed, printed typescript, faxed, and autograph materials. Includes drafts of proposals for "Star Wars, Tales of the Jedi," some with autograph annotation, along with autograph notes on projects. A substantial portion of the binder is devoted to outgoing correspondence with Lucy Wilson, of Lucasfilm, and Barbara Kesel, of Dark Horse Comics, among others. Some incoming correspondence from Wilson and Kesel is present, primarily containing critiques and responses to Veitch's proposed works. Veitch has laid in sticky notes throughout to contextualize certain items.

did.unittitle: Dark Empire and Tales of the Jedi correspondence with LucasArts and Dark Horse Comics

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing approx. 1.5 linear inches of outgoing correspondence, including typewritten, faxed, and printed letters, signed, and Xeroxed typescript with the majority consisting of printed typescript and faxed typescript. Correspondents include Lucy Wilson of Lucasfilm, and Barbara Kesel and Kij Johnson of Dark Horse Comics, among others. Correspondence relates Veitch's outlines and plot proposals for issues of "Star Wars: Dark Empire." Autograph annotations appear on selected outlines, and some autograph notes are present. Includes shipping receipts for hardcopy documents sent to Dark Horse Comics and approximately 20 work orders issued to Veitch by Dark Horse Comics.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Approx. 1.5 linear inches of printed electronic messages, both incoming and outgoing, related to "Star Wars," regarding Veitch's work on the movies. Correspondents include Skip Shayotovich, Michael Horne, Chris Duemmel, Robert Quillen, Mark Wandrey, Alan Grimes, John Jones, Bill Smith, Mike Hutchings, and Ron Golaszewski, among others. Veitch has underlined and annotated messages in red pen throughout.

did.unittitle: Dark Empire II and TOTJ Dark Lords of the Sith correspondence with Dark Horse Comics and Lucasfilm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing correspondence with Dark Horse and Lucasfilm, primarily regarding story rights, royalties, and publishing negotiations between Veitch and the respective companies. Includes Xeroxed and faxed typewritten letter, signed, with autograph notes, drafts, and annotations throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Xeroxed printed typescript correspondence with Lucasfilm and Dark Horse Comics, much of it related to plot approvals and feedback on delivered story lines; Xeroxed fax materials includes approvals from Lucasfilm, many answered by Lucas, at times in his hand, for Veitch's proposed additions to the "Star Wars" universe; autograph notes by Veitch present along with corner-stapled autograph proposal for "Star Wars, Tales of the Jedi," annotated by Veitch. Approx. half of materials are housed in manila folder titled by Veitch: "Stuff for SW/LFL Binders," in which Veitch has included shipping slips to document hardcopy materials sent to Dark Horse Comics.

did.unittitle: Contract discussions with LucasArts and Dark Horse Comics related to Dark Empire II and Tales of the Jedi

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing approx. 1.5 linear inches of outgoing and incoming correspondence related to "Star Wars: Dark Empire," often in response to contract negotiations and translations of the works. Contains printed, Xeroxed, faxed, and printed electronic correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence between Veitch and collaborator/co-writer Kevin J. Anderson related to "Star Wars, Tales of the Jedi: Dark Lords of the Sith." Includes 2 typewritten letters, signed, from Veitch to Anderson; Xeroxed typewritten letters, signed, from Veitch to Anderson with autograph annotations; typewritten letter from Veitch to Anderson; and typewritten letter, signed, from Anderson to Veitch including brief packet of background documents for character development research.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed typescripts and Xeroxed printed typescripts, some with handwritten revisions throughout, primarily including correspondence related to the Time Warner adaptation of "Star Wars: Dark Empire" into audiobook format. Contains drafts of audiobook scripts with Veitch's edits and annotations, and shipping slip to John Whitman of Time Warner from Veitch.

did.unittitle: Dark Empire II and TOTJ Dark Lords of the Sith correspondence with Dark Horse Comics and Lucasfilm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing approximately 1 linear inch of outgoing and incoming correspondence with Dark Horse Comics and Lucasfilm. Primarily consists of Xeroxed , faxed, and typewritten letters, signed and unsigned. Some ephemera inserted by Veitch, including signed Certificate of Authenticity for "Star Wars, Tales of the Jedi: Gold Foil" comic set.

did.unittitle: Dark Empire II and TOTJ Dark Lords of the Sith correspondence with Dark Horse Comics and Lucasfilm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black plastic three-ring binder, assembled by Veitch, containing approximately 1 linear inch of outgoing and incoming correspondence with Dark Horse Comics and Lucasfilm. Primarily consists of Xeroxed , faxed, and typewritten letters, signed and unsigned. Some ephemera inserted by Veitch, including signed Certificate of Authenticity for "Star Wars, Tales of the Jedi: Gold Foil" comic set.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is arranged alphabetically and divided into four subseries: Early works, comic projects, Star Wars , and esoteric and spiritual writings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains project files and drafts of published and unpublished writings by Veitch.

Early works mainly feature materials from the 1960s-1970s, with the notable exceptions being a 1950s file of comics drawn during Veitch's youth and later documents that have been interfiled with earlier works. This subseries documents the wide range of Veitch's literary activities that took place after his college years and prior to his success as a mainstream comic writer. Many of these works feature small-press collaborations with counterculture writers and poets in New York and San Francisco. Featured novels include Star Gut (unpublished, written 1968) with Ron Padgett, Antlers in the Treetops (Toronto: Coach House Press, 1973) with Ron Padgett, The Planetary Route (unpublished, written 1969) with Dick Gallup, The Luis Armed Story (New York: Full Court Press, 1978 and Berlin: Köln, Kiepenheuer & Witsch: 1970), and Eat This! A Story (New York: Angel Hair Books, 1974). Published poetry anthologies include Cooked Zeros (Stinson Beach, California: Grape Press, 1970), My Father's Golden Eye (in Adventures in Poetry , 1970), Death College (New York: Siamese Banana Press, 1970), and Death College and Other Poems (Bolinas, California: Big Sky, and Berkeley, California: Serendipity Books, 1976). Other notable works include In the Foundry , an unpublished stage play co-written with Ted Berrigan and Ron Padgett, as well as drafts of the four-issue Tom Veitch Magazine (self-published, 1970-1971).

Comic projects document Veitch's career as a comic book writer through project files containing drafts in the forms of scripts, book dummies, and, frequently, communication with editors and collaborators. Materials generally date from the 1980s-1990s and do not include Veitch's works in the Star Wars franchise. Pre-1988 works in this subseries generally reflect Veitch's participation in the Underground Comix movement. These include contributions to horror anthologies such as Gore Shriek (FantaCo Enterprises), Death Rattle (Kitchen Sink Press), and Taboo (Kitchen Sink Press). Several works in this category include Veitch's collaborations with tattoo and comic artist Greg Irons, such as Deviant Slice (Print Mint, 1972-1973), The Legion of Charlies (Last Gasp, 1971), Grunt (Grunt Records, 1972-1973), and Flenk's Last Tattoo (unpublished, written 1980s). An unpublished comic from this time, The First Man and The Last Man , is also present. Veitch's work with mainstream comics is documented from his work on The Light and Darkness War (Marvel's Epic Comics, 1988) onwards. Several of Veitch's works for DC Comics were published during this time, including one-off and ongoing series like Kamandi , Superman , Clash (1991), Swamp Thing , and The Nazz (1990-1991). Key collaborators for these mainstream works include Cam Kennedy, Bryan Talbot, John Ridgway, and Adam Kubert.

Star Wars contains project files for Star Wars comics. All published works were licensed by Lucasfilm and published by Dark Horse Comics in the 1990s. The first of these works was Dark Empire (1991-1992), produced in collaboration with The Light and Darkness War (1988) artist Cam Kennedy. This serialization resulted in a continuing storyline through Dark Empire II (1994-1995) and Empire's End (1995). Veitch's other major published Star Wars work was Tales of the Jedi (1993-1994), co-written with Kevin J. Anderson. Veitch continued work on this storyline through Tales of the Jedi: Dark Lords of the Sith (1994-1995) and contributed early concept work for Tales of the Jedi: The Sith War (1995-1996), although he was never officially credited for this final work. Two notable unpublished works are documented in this subseries. One is the cancelled Lightsider novel from the mid-1990s. The other is an incomplete autobiography in which Veitch reflects on his prior work with the Star Wars franchise; this autobiography -- substantial draft material for which is retained in the digital files within Series IX -- is tentatively entitled Imagining Star Wars: How We Expanded George Lucas' Universe . Additional materials related to Veitch's writing process and collaborations for Star Wars projects are available in Series I, IV, and VI.

Esoteric and spiritual writings contain drafts of works that evoke Veitch's spirituality, often under his pseudonym "Elias Oz." Most are deeply personal in character and, apart from the biography Visions of Elias: A True Story of Life in the Spirit (Manchester, Vermont: Sky River Books, 2016), remain unpublished. A notable work documented here is Visions of a Poet , an unpublished autobiography written by Veitch during the 1970s. Other materials include early essays on spirituality, dream summaries, and documentation related to Veitch's participation in spiritually inclined internet forums in the 1990s and 2000s. Substantial material related to Veitch's spirituality is present in Series III and Series IX.
For additional materials related to writings, see notebooks in Series IV and artworks in Series VIII.

did.unittitle: Antlers in the Treetops , with Ron Padgett

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Envelope from Bernstein with unbound proof pages, postmarked 2009.

did.unittitle: Assorted manuscripts, including prose drafts and literary reviews

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Several typescripts, including review of "The Job" by W.S. Burroughs; typescript fragment on Greg Irons; "The Trapdoor Method" on teaching; "Untitled M.S. Found in a Garbage Can" by A. Veitch; "The Novels of Tom Veitch" with significant holograph notes, likely not by Veitch; and printed typescript review of Ron Padgett's "How to Be Perfect."

did.unittitle: Burroughs Dim Far Away , typescript draft and correspondence with Janine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 manila envelopes, one addressed from [George] Mattingly of Blue Wind Press (publisher of W.S. Burroughs), containing autograph and paste-up bound copy of the manuscript, and another envelope with camera copy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 manila envelopes, one addressed from [George] Mattingly of Blue Wind Press (publisher of W.S. Burroughs), containing autograph and paste-up bound copy of the manuscript, and another envelope with camera copy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Several typescript collaborations between Veitch and Andrei Codrescu; "The Naked Express" cut-up by "Tom Veitch and Willy," which was a C Press related piece of ephemera created solely by Veitch; and sheet of "Star Spangled" by "Tom Veitch and Willy" with cut-ups, attributed to Veitch and Padgett, as well as Anne Waldman (printed on newsprint).

did.unittitle: Death College / Death College & Other Poems

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Xeroxed version of this collaboration between Veitch, Dick Gallup, Joe Brainard, George Schneeman, and others, printed by Siamese Banana Press in 1970.

did.unittitle: Various drafts in autograph, typescript, printout, and paste-up

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 5 manila envelopes with draft materials for "Death College" by Siamese Banana Press, and also "Death College and Other Poems," which included "Death College," "Toad Poems," "Cooked Zeroes," "My Father's Golden Eye," and "Tom Veitch Magazine," Veitch's mimeographed publications. Manila envelope addressed to Grape Press, titled "Death College and Other Poems," includes paste-ups, correspondence, typescript and carbon typescript drafts of poems, autograph notes, autograph and typescript drafts of poems, paste-ups for other works, autobiographical drafts, and poetry manuscripts by Veitch. Other envelopes include materials for "Death College and Other Poems," with paste-ups, typescript and Xeroxed material, and an envelope with typewritten letter, signed, from Bill Berkson (October 2 1976) with camera copy of "Death College" as well as paste-ups of "Last Word" collage-poem. There is also an envelope from Anne Waldman with autograph letter, signed, enclosing an early reading copy/draft of "Death College and Other Poems" (with title "American Bodies"), accompanied by typewritten letter, signed, from Bill Berkson to Allen Ginsberg, and 5 pp. of extensive holograph notes by Allen Ginsberg on the manuscript.

did.unittitle: Various drafts in autograph, typescript, printout, and paste-up

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 5 manila envelopes with draft materials for "Death College" by Siamese Banana Press, and also "Death College and Other Poems," which included "Death College," "Toad Poems," "Cooked Zeroes," "My Father's Golden Eye," and "Tom Veitch Magazine," Veitch's mimeographed publications. Manila envelope addressed to Grape Press, titled "Death College and Other Poems," includes paste-ups, correspondence, typescript and carbon typescript drafts of poems, autograph notes, autograph and typescript drafts of poems, paste-ups for other works, autobiographical drafts, and poetry manuscripts by Veitch. Other envelopes include materials for "Death College and Other Poems," with paste-ups, typescript and Xeroxed material, and an envelope with typewritten letter, signed, from Bill Berkson (October 2 1976) with camera copy of "Death College" as well as paste-ups of "Last Word" collage-poem. There is also an envelope from Anne Waldman with autograph letter, signed, enclosing an early reading copy/draft of "Death College and Other Poems" (with title "American Bodies"), accompanied by typewritten letter, signed, from Bill Berkson to Allen Ginsberg, and 5 pp. of extensive holograph notes by Allen Ginsberg on the manuscript.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder assembled by Veitch with typescript drafts of early works, many with handwritten revisions. Includes "The Erasure," "The Wedding," "Too Bad," and others. Most poems are titled but not dated.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder with several title sheets of Veitch's work, as well as typescript drafts of prose and poetry pieces. Folder appears to have been assembled as a compilation, perhaps for Veitch's first collected book of poetry. There is also significant autograph draft material, including notes and side-stapled storyboard script of "The Silver Surfer," an unpublished early comic.

did.unittitle: Early prose, poetry, essay, and stage play

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A substantial folder for Veitch's early poetry writings and "Tom Veitch Magazine." Contains numerous poetry drafts, "Antlers in the Treetops," "Super Bart," a manuscript of "Untitled Poems for Clark Coolidge" by Martha Veitch, several dozen typescripts of poems and short pieces with handwritten revisions, a two-page comic with Rick Veitch, collaborations with Ron Padgett, essay on underground comix, poetry reading introduction to Veitch, and other works. There is also a short play, as well as pages and paste-ups from "Tom Veitch Magazine." Version of "My Heart is Coo-Coo for God," attributed to "Grape Press 1971" (this work was published also in "Tom Veitch Magazine"). Typescript, carbon typescript, and autograph material that foregrounds Veitch's early collaborations.

did.unittitle: Eat This! , three typescript and carbon typescript drafts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 envelopes taped together by Veitch. Includes 4 versions of the manuscript in typescript and carbon typescript form, including one "c/o Ron Padgett." Many autograph annotations throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 issues of "Flag Comics," nos. 1–3. This was a hand-drawn comic, attributed to "Veitch," and likely created by the young Veitches collaboratively (per the juvenile handwriting). With drawings and activities (such as mazes, puzzles, connect-the-dots, and jokes).

did.unittitle: Grinning Boy Who Lets the Wind Come and Go , screenplay drafts by Veitch and Joe Wemple

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 copies in green, yellow, and blue folders of screenplay draft by Joe Wemple and Veitch. Green and yellow drafts are dated July 24 1970; blue folder contains draft with significantly more handwritten revisions and annotations throughout. With some handwritten notes laid in.

did.unittitle: Grinning Boy Who Lets the Wind Come and Go , screenplay drafts by Veitch and Joe Wemple

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 copies in green, yellow, and blue folders of screenplay draft by Joe Wemple and Veitch. Green and yellow drafts are dated July 24 1970; blue folder contains draft with significantly more handwritten revisions and annotations throughout. With some handwritten notes laid in.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder labeled "Grist," with Xeroxed materials used for cut-up collaborations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila envelope with horoscopes drawn up by Veitch for his friends, with detailed astrological notes. Includes autograph and typescript charts for Andrei Codrescu, Darrell [?], Bill Berkson, Rick Veitch, Michael Brownstein, Lewis Warsh, Clark Coolidge, Bill Presson, Nancy Presson, Hugh McKennon, and John Clark.

did.unittitle: In the Foundry , with Ted Berrigan and Ron Padgett, typescript, carbon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4 pp. carbon typescript of play with holograph annotations. The end of the manuscript notes: "written after dinner in 1965 by Ted Berrigan, Ron Padgett, & Tom Veitch, with additional dialogue by Dick Gallup."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 5 manila envelopes and 1 folder, related to the drafting and production of "The Luis Armed Story." Folder contains carbon typescript draft of work with significant handwritten revisions, and note on inside cover of bound manuscript "164 pages, copy sent to B.H." Envelopes contain typescript and carbon typescript drafts, "first" copy with notes on revisions in 1969, 1972, and 1973, the Full Court Press control edition copy, and two envelopes from Verlag Kiepenheuer & Witsch, the initial German publisher of the work, with reviews and typescript from the publisher circa 1965.

did.unittitle: Manuscript and clippings from German publisher
"Reviews of Luis Armed," related to German publication
Bound typescript, likely an early draft

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black binder with extensive (1.5 linear inch) early draft of "The Luis Armed Story," featuring introduction by "Carl Jung." Manuscript is a typescript draft with handwritten revisions as well as cut and paste sections throughout; "Jung's" introduction is a Xerox copy.

did.unittitle: "Luis Armed Full Court [Press] edition control copy," typescript

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila envelopes for this Adventures in Poetry publication, with 5 pp. autograph poems and notes laid in on back page.

did.unittitle: My Heart is Coo-Coo For God , pamphlet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript and autograph material, mostly prose works including draft fragments of "The First Man & The Last Man" with significant autograph edits; typescript extensively annotated on the psychology of guruism; prose piece on "Close Encounters"; and poem for Allen Ginsberg accompanied by typewritten letter, signed, from Bill Morgan in 1984. With some autograph poems. Contains typescript draft on "Helldiver" character and plot sketch.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila folder with Veitch's autograph drawings on cover. Contains typescripts of poems from 1982–1983, with handwritten revisions and notes on where they were submitted and published, as well as paste-ups for publications, edited drafts of prose works (including on Jack Kerouac), and some handwritten notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila folder with Veitch's autograph drawings on cover. Contains typescripts of poems from 1982–1983, with handwritten revisions and notes on where they were submitted and published, as well as paste-ups for publications, edited drafts of prose works (including on Jack Kerouac), and some handwritten notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder labeled by Veitch. Contains several typescript fragments, typewritten letter, signed, from Richard Kostelanetz, and typescript draft of "Ernest Hemingway's Hangnail."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 manila envelopes with manuscripts and fragments, one of which is in envelope from Toothpaste Press. In typescript, carbon typescript, and handwritten versions. Includes four poems inscribed to Jim [Gustafson], and fragments with cut-and-paste revisions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 manila envelopes with manuscripts and fragments, one of which is in envelope from Toothpaste Press. In typescript, carbon typescript, and handwritten versions. Includes four poems inscribed to Jim [Gustafson], and fragments with cut-and-paste revisions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder with "Ron—here's more grist for XEROX-ing. Tom" on cover. This refers to the poets' practice of exchanging envelopes of Xeroxed material for cut-up collaborations. Contains typescript poems with handwritten revisions from 1970, and signature removed from composition notebook titled "Enter at Your Own Risk," with extensive autograph manuscript of mostly poetry.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder labeled by Veitch. With title pages of "Book of Dreams" and "The Only Bible," and prose fragments within.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed typescript for a selection from "Antlers in the Treetops" and "Ernest Hemingway's Hangnail" with speaking parts for both Padgett and Veitch; clipping of Tom Clark poem "Joe"; and typescript with handwritten revisions of "Yes, I am William Burroughs" by Veitch. Accompanied by two reference pieces on Burroughs' cut-up poetry method.

did.unittitle: Selected texts for Visiting Poetics Academy course at Naropa Institute

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila envelope with "Visiting Poetics Academy Naropa Institute" packet, containing selected texts for study for course by Veitch. With carbon copy typescripts of poetry alongside esoteric and Eastern philosophical works.

did.unittitle: "Star Gut (original mss)" with later note from Ron Padgett

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folder with autograph note by Ron Padgett: "Old Bean, this is the best photocopy I could get. Xox Ron." Contains photocopy of their early collaboration, "Star Gut." Padgett remembers: "Over the course of a week we wrote alternate pages of a science fiction novella entitled 'Star Gut' (a title taken from a collaborative painting by George Schneeman, Tom, and me, in which the words 'Arlo Guthrie' had been altered to 'Star Gut'). There was much chuckling at the typewriter as Tom and I worked; later we were told by an experienced sci-fi writer that no publisher would take on the manuscript because science fiction isn't supposed to be funny." This text was the precursor to their next collaboration, "Antlers in the Treetops."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Red binder with manuscript of "Star Gut," by Ron Padgett and Veitch. 1 linear inch of printed typescript with some foxing.

did.unittitle: Ted Berrigan, A Memory , autograph and typescript drafts
The Homeless Only Die Once, Right? , typescript

A cut-and-paste work, with autograph colophon on back page that notes collaborations with Ron Padgett, Dick Gallup, and Lewis MacAdams.

did.unittitle: Unidentified screenplay, typescript draft with revisions
Untitled bound compilation of early writings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black binder with compilation of Veitch's prose works, with autograph table of contents, and Xeroxed, typescript, and paste-up elements. Works included are "The Tom Veitch Story (includes 'Literary Days')," "The Message from You Now Jack Kerouac," "The Words (excerpt + pope's message)," "Them Rolling Hills," "My Heart is Coo-Coo for God," "Strawberries (w. D. Gallup from 'Planetary Route')," "Antlers in the Treetops (excerpt)," "To Obtain the Value of the Cake Measure from Zero (with C.C.)," "The Hippie Termites (w. A. Codrescu chaps. 1–5)," "Star Gut (novel with R. Padgett)." At back, contains carbon typescript of manuscripts and books sold to Louis Collins (a West coast bookdealer) in 1978, including several collaborations between Veitch and Andrei Codrescu.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manila envelope labeled by Veitch, with 6 pp. handwritten poem, "The Journal of My Days Away from You," dated January 1962 (Veitch was 20). Includes several additional typescript poems, some with handwritten revisions.

With original drawings by Angelica (some annotation by Veitch) and copies of drawings, as well as includes typewritten letter to Adam [Kubert?] with a newspaper comic strip proposal, inspired by Angelica's creation.

did.unittitle: "Image War + online G. Nov.," reference material for online graphic novel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, notes, script drafts, storyboards, printed proofs, and project proposal materials for "Kamandi: Earth's End," with Adam Kubert as illustrator. Materials are typescript, printed typescript, and holograph.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed materials from "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 1, a Veitch collaboration with artist Frank Gomez for DC Comics. Includes 26 pp. side-stapled mockup with autograph panels, accompanied by autograph panel layouts; several printed typescript drafts of script; Xeroxed mock-up drafts with artwork and dialogue; autograph notes; typescript correspondence from Veitch to Frank Gomez and Mike Carlin, of DC Comics, discussing edits and revisions to scripts and drafts; incomplete Xerox drafts of artwork; and printed typescript for "Thunder Road," which would become issue no. 3. Significant handwritten annotations throughout on almost all materials.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed material from "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 2. Includes autograph panel layout; printed typescript drafts of script (including letterer's script, which was prepared for letterer); Xeroxed drafts of artwork by Frank Gomez; and Xeroxed typewritten letter, signed, from Veitch to Mike Carlin. There are autograph annotations throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed materials related to "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 3. Includes autograph panel layout and printed typescript draft of letterer's script along with 2 Xeroxed, side-stapled mock-up dummies with artwork and dialogue. Numerous Xeroxed drafts of artwork by Gomez present along with Xeroxed typewritten letter, signed, from Veitch to Gomez. Significant handwritten annotations and revisions throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed materials from "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 4. Includes autograph panel layouts, printed typescript summaries/revisions of pages, printed typescript draft of script, and side-stapled Xeroxed mock-up dummy with artwork and dialogue. Xeroxed drafts of artwork by Frank Gomez included. Significant autograph annotations and emendations throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed materials related to "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 5. Includes autograph panel layouts, printed typescript page summaries, FedEx shipping slips, Xeroxed printed typescript letterer's script, Xeroxed printed typescript draft of script, and side-stapled Xeroxed mock-up dummy. Handwritten annotations and revisions throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph, printed typescript, and Xeroxed materials related to "Kamandi: At Earth's End," no. 6. Includes autograph panel layouts, printed typescript drafts of scripts, letterer's scripts, and Xeroxed drafts of artwork layout with note specifying "for bubbling." With additional Xeroxed drafts of Frank Gomez artwork. Handwritten annotations and revisions throughout.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed typescript, typescript, and manuscript material, often with extensive annotations, regarding the development of "The Light & Darkness War," a comic project with Cam Kennedy in six books, published by Marvel Comics' distinguished imprint, Epic Comics. These files trace the earliest discussions about the project in autograph notes, and up to nine versions of each book, showing the extensive narrative design that went into the series. With hand-drawn mockup storyboards; a comprehensive folder of incoming and outgoing correspondence between Cam Kennedy (illustrator) and Veitch with many holograph letters, as well as correspondence with studios/publishers; typescript drafts of scripts and plots; notes (many holograph); printed proofs

did.unittitle: Veitch, "Intruders at the Gates of the Mind: Horror in the Undergrounds 1968-1975," Gore Shriek no. 5

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript essay by Veitch titled "Intruders at the Gates of the Mind: Horror in the Undergrounds 1968–1975," an essay published in "Gore Shriek" that explores R. Crumb, S. Clay Wilson, and other underground comix creators. With typewritten letter, signed, from Tom to "Gore Shriek" in 1988, and additional autograph drafting notes with 2 pp. typescript on S. Clay Wilson. Important writing on the influence of gore and horror for some of the most influential comix illustrators in this experimental genre.
